author: @3D_Printing
text: remember to give warnings about resin

author: @YearRoundHibernater
text:
I've had one for just over a year, few tips I picked up. 
I use a drop of PTFE lube on the resin tray wipe it round really helps the resin not stick to the fep sheet.
If you do get parts sticking to the fep sheet, always remove them carefully, always with the plastic scrapper never the metal one or you risk puncturing it.
Always wear gloves it's a hell of a messy thing to work with if it spills
If you plan on using it a lot look into getting a magnet flexible spring steel bed, you can remove it bend it and makes getting prints off so so much easier, also can give it a quick rub over with some rough sand paper and it helps adhesion.
If you have a failed print always empty your resin tray completely, you don't want a solid piece left in there being forced down when you start your next print and it puncturing the fep and your resin spilling every where.
And a metal coffee filter for drip coffee works great as a filter rather buying disposable ones all the time.

author: @kdog8787
text: Hi Rob, I've used that ABS-Like resin a lot and found that it degrades very quickly just above 60C. I've found that it can withstand 2M NaOH for days (I haven't tried for longer).

author: @PhilWaud
text: Its great to see your channel growing! In your vid, you talk about the resolution of filament 3d printers being limited to the size of the filament. This isnt quite correct! The filament is extruded through a nozzle which can be as small as 0.1mm but is commonly 0.4mm. Clearly, this leads to your 3d print having a series of steps on in in the xy direction. What really defines the "resolution" is the size and accuracy of the steps in the xy and z direction. These are all controlled by the software and limited by the hardware but can easily be as low as 0.1mm. The smaller the step size, the longer your model takes which can be very frustrating if you want anything large. To solve this, manufacturers have come up with much larger nozzles and much higher capacity heating elements which massively increase the size of model whilst reducing the print time. James Bruton has a Youtube channel and printed a double life size model of himself!

replies.author: @kdog8787
replies.text: You're right about the nozzle determining the smallest possible detail in the print, but the "resolution" that you're talking about is for the motion of the extruder, not the actual print. Companies use that "resolution" for marketing but it is irrelevant for any decent filament printer. You don't see steps in the xy direction on prints from a decent printer. The "resolution" that Rob is talking about is for the actual print which is better compared to the nozzle size on a filament printer albeit not the same.

author: @edeniaAJ
text:
The main downside of resin printers is that they use thermosets, which are far more difficult to recycle. FDM can be precise, and even get to layer heights smaller than 0.1mm. The filament isn't really the limiting factor, its moreso that you're working with materials that are super soft and deformable when molten, combined with using a clunky gantry (especially with heavier print-heads).

Nevertheless, the resolution of SLA and MSLA are unmatched. I just wish there was as much information about resin formulations as there are filaments. What's more exciting in this area is recent developments in bio-based resins, like those made from Epoxidsed soybean oil. When ESO is acrylated and mixed with a photoinitiator, let's say benzophenone (easily attainable from heating calcium benzoate), it can make a bio-derieved resin. I guess this can be mixed with pigments and additives such as plasticisers to liking.

Keep up the good work as always, and looking forward to the review!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: thank you mate and nice tips on a way forward with our own resin formulations

author: @daveofyorkshire301
text: Isn't the cover to protect the print not your eyes? Its designed to keep natural UV out of the print area whilst its running for a few hours... Your eyes are perfectly safe unless you're splashing resin in them...

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I would guess both mate UV is not that good for the eyes I believe

replies.author: @daveofyorkshire301
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering UV is part of natural light, its an essential life element, as it aids the conversion of vitamin C to vitamin D, its why you get ill if you don't get enough sun. Your eyes are more than capable of dealing with the relatively low levels an obscured screen (with resin) could ever put out... Unless you're planning on staring into it without the reservoir there, and why would you even have it on then?

A print takes long enough that natural light would be curing the top of the resin camber as it prints, hence the need to block UV light...

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @daveofyorkshire301 sorry mate - that is a bit of a spurious argument - the sun is essential to life - but you wouldn't want to go staring at it - too much of anything is bad for you - you eat an egg - no worries - but try eating 50 and you would be in trouble. The idea that anything natural is good for is also a bit odd - Snake venom is natural but I wouldn't want to drink a glass of it - cheers

replies.author: @daveofyorkshire301
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Strange how just about everyone is happy to sit/lay on a beach and bask in it...

I've even got blacklight UV bulbs in the house to compensate fir winter low light levels, and i don't shelter my eyes from them either, primarily because you cant see anything, but tell nightclubs they use blacklight a lot too... You're probably exposed a lot more than you think... A 7" (at best) low power phone screen is NOT going to harm anyone, the only danger with those printers is the resin itself...

replies.author: @____________________________.x
replies.text: I gather that the unused resin is just left sitting in the tray until required for the next print, and that’s why it has the red shroud to stop it curing in the tray from the sun. Apparently it’s OK just to leave in there for weeks? I agree that the UV LEDs are going to be less of an issue because although they are powerful, they are focused just on the tray, and there’s that moving platform sitting above it anyway, but yes you would need the shield to cover that anyway. The smell from the UV reaction process is quite hazardous though, these things need a fume cupboard I think
